title: Ceviche de Camaron Shrimp Lime
description: This vibrant Ceviche de Camaron is a refreshing symphony of flavors, where succulent shrimp is marinated in bright lime juice, then tossed with crisp vegetables like tomato, onion, and cilantro. It’s a beloved coastal Mexican dish, offering a perfect balance of zesty, savory, and a hint of spice, ideal for a warm day.

ingredients.notes: finely minced, deseeded for milder heat
peeled, seeded, finely diced (optional, but traditional in some regions)

instructions.title: Prepare the Shrimp
instructions.text: Clean, peel, devein, and remove tails from the raw shrimp. Rinse thoroughly under cold water and pat dry with paper towels. Cut the shrimp into small, bite-sized pieces, roughly 1/2 inch, for even 'cooking' and easier serving.

instructions.title: Marinate the Shrimp (Cocido en Limón)
instructions.text: Place the chopped shrimp in a non-reactive bowl (glass or ceramic). Squeeze the juice from all the fresh limes directly over the shrimp, ensuring it is fully submerged. Stir gently to coat. Cover the bowl and refrigerate for at least 20-30 minutes, or until the shrimp turns opaque and pink throughout, indicating it has 'cooked' in the acid. Stir occasionally to ensure even marination.

instructions.title: Prepare the Vegetables
instructions.text: While the shrimp marinates, prepare your vegetables. Finely dice the Roma tomatoes (removing seeds helps reduce excess moisture), red onion, and cucumber (if using). Finely mince the serrano peppers; remove seeds and veins for a milder heat, or leave some for more spice. Chop the fresh cilantro.

instructions.title: Combine and Season
instructions.text: Once the shrimp is opaque, gently drain about half to three-quarters of the excess lime juice from the bowl, leaving enough to keep the mixture moist but not soupy. Add the diced tomatoes, red onion, cucumber, minced serrano peppers, and chopped cilantro to the shrimp.

instructions.title: Flavor Integration and Chill
instructions.text: Stir all the ingredients together thoroughly to combine. Season generously with salt and freshly ground black pepper to taste. Mix well. For optimal flavor, cover the bowl and refrigerate for another 15-20 minutes to allow the flavors to meld and deepen.

instructions.title: Serve Authentic Mexican Style
instructions.text: Taste and adjust seasoning if necessary. Serve the Ceviche de Camaron chilled, traditionally piled high on crispy tostadas or alongside saltine crackers (galletas saladas). Garnish each serving with fresh diced avocado just before eating. Offer your favorite Mexican hot sauce on the side for those who prefer an extra kick.

tips: **Freshness is Key:** Always use the freshest shrimp and limes you can find. The quality of these ingredients directly impacts the flavor and safety of your ceviche.
**Lime Juice Quantity:** Ensure the shrimp is fully submerged in lime juice. If not, add more. The acid is what 'cooks' the shrimp, and insufficient juice can lead to uneven texture.
**Don't Overcook:** Over-marinating the shrimp in lime juice can make it rubbery. Check for opacity; once it's pink and firm, it's ready for mixing with vegetables.
**Balance the Heat:** For less spice, remove the seeds and veins from the serrano peppers, or use fewer peppers. For more heat, leave them in or add an extra one.
**Chill Thoroughly:** Ceviche is best served very cold. Chilling it for an extra 15-20 minutes after mixing allows the flavors to deepen and meld beautifully.
article:
## History of Ceviche de Camaron Shrimp Lime

Ceviche, while found across Latin America, has deep roots in Mexican coastal regions, particularly along the Pacific. The technique of "cooking" seafood in citrus juice dates back centuries, potentially influenced by indigenous practices and later by Spanish culinary traditions. In Mexico, specifically, ceviche de camaron is a staple in states like Sinaloa, Sonora, and Nayarit, where fresh seafood is abundant. It's a testament to resourcefulness and the vibrant flavors of the land and sea, offering a refreshing and light meal perfect for warm climates. Each region often has its unique twist, but the core elements of fresh shrimp, lime, and crisp vegetables remain constant, embodying the spirit of Mexican coastal cuisine.

## Why This Recipe Works

This recipe thrives on the principle of "cocción ácida," or acid cooking. The high acidity of fresh lime juice denatures the proteins in the shrimp, effectively "cooking" it without heat, resulting in a tender yet firm texture. The careful balance of fresh, crisp vegetables—sweet tomatoes, pungent red onion, sharp cilantro, and fiery serrano—provides a perfect counterpoint to the bright, tangy shrimp. The slight draining of excess lime juice ensures the ceviche isn't overly watery, allowing the individual flavors to shine through while still retaining that essential zesty punch. It's a masterclass in fresh ingredients and simple, effective technique.

## Chef Tips for Perfection

To achieve truly exceptional ceviche, start with absolutely pristine, sushi-grade shrimp. Its freshness is non-negotiable for both flavor and safety. When dicing vegetables, aim for uniform, small pieces; this ensures every spoonful offers a balanced medley of textures and tastes. Don't rush the lime marination—give the shrimp adequate time to "cook," but also don't let it sit for hours, which can toughen it. A key authentic touch is using Mexican limes (limones), which are smaller, tarter, and more aromatic than Persian limes. Finally, a pinch of good quality sea salt is crucial for enhancing all the other flavors.

## Serving Suggestions

Ceviche de Camaron is most authentically served piled high on crispy tostadas, often with a thin layer of mayonnaise or a spread of mashed avocado underneath for added creaminess. Alternatively, serve it in chilled bowls with a side of saltine crackers (galletas saladas) for scooping. A drizzle of your favorite Mexican hot sauce, such as Valentina or Tapatío, is almost mandatory for an extra kick. For a complete meal, pair it with a cold cerveza, a refreshing agua fresca, or a crisp white wine. It also makes an excellent appetizer for a larger Mexican feast.

jsonLd.name: Ceviche de Camaron Shrimp Lime
jsonLd.description: This vibrant Ceviche de Camaron is a refreshing symphony of flavors, where succulent shrimp is marinated in bright lime juice, then tossed with crisp vegetables like tomato, onion, and cilantro. It’s a beloved coastal Mexican dish, offering a perfect balance of zesty, savory, and a hint of spice, ideal for a warm day.
jsonLd.keywords: Mexican, Mexican, authentic, traditional, seafood, Mexican, Seafood, authentic, traditional, Lime, Shrimp, Ceviche
recipeIngredient: 1.5 lbs Fresh raw shrimp (peeled, deveined, tail-off, sushi-grade recommended)
10-12 medium Limes (freshly squeezed, Mexican limes preferred)
1-2 Serrano peppers (finely minced, deseeded for milder heat)
1/2 English cucumber (peeled, seeded, finely diced (optional, but traditional in some regions))
1/2 tsp Black pepper (freshly ground, or to taste)
as needed Tostadas or Saltine crackers (for serving)
to taste Mexican hot sauce (e.g., Valentina, Tapatío (optional))
